summary:
The Panathenaic Games (reorganized and expanded c. 566 BC, under the tyrant [[event:3097|Peisistratus Athens]] Pisistratus) were Athens's premier civic festival in honor of the goddess Athena — held annually, with a Greater Panathenaia every four years that rivaled the Olympic Games in prestige. The festival combined athletic competitions, musical and rhapsodic contests (recitation of Homer), and culminated in the Panathenaic procession depicted on the Parthenon frieze, in which a new robe (peplos) was carried to the Acropolis to dress the ancient olive-wood statue of Athena.

The Panathenaic Games were distinctly Athenian in character: unlike the Panhellenic games (Olympic, Pythian, Isthmian, Nemean), non-Athenians competed for valuable prizes — large amphorae filled with sacred olive oil — rather than mere wreaths. The amphorae (Panathenaic prize amphorae) depicted athletic events and were themselves works of art; hundreds survive in museums worldwide. The festival was a powerful instrument of Athenian civic identity: it unified the diverse population of Attica around worship of Athena, displayed Athenian prosperity and artistic achievement, and the procession itself was a collective performance of civic community. The Parthenon frieze's famous depiction of the procession may be the only example of contemporary Athenian civic life in Classical Greek sculpture.
